India is quietly building the 𝐨𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐬𝐲𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐦 for AI-first finance.


If your fintech or bank is still running static workflows while UPI, AA, and domain AI models go agentic, you’re leaving serious leverage on the table.

We’re past the “AI as a feature” phase.
From UPI rails and account aggregators to FiMI and voice-led banking, India is proving that AI is now core infrastructure for financial services. But here’s the catch: most BFSI stacks in India still look like this—solid data pipelines, great product features, and a growing pile of isolated AI pilots that never become workflows.

Meanwhile, customers expect instant decisions, contextual nudges, and personalised journeys across lending, payments, and wealth.

This is where hybrid, agentic workflows change the game.
Instead of running yet another offline model or launching yet another chatbot, leading teams are:
🔹Using batch and streaming layers for heavy lifting: risk scores, segmentation, fraud signals, income patterns.
🔹Exposing those as tools – “check KYC risk”, “simulate cashflow”, “compute blended credit limit” – that agents can call on demand.
🔹Letting agents orchestrate real workflows: underwriting flows, collections strategies, cross-sell offers, and dispute resolutions, step by step.

This is how AI moves from PoC decks to live business logic in Indian BFSI.
Not another bot on the website, but an orchestration layer that sits on top of India’s digital public infra and your existing systems to drive approvals, decisions, and nudges in real time.

If you’re a CXO or product/data leader in India’s BFSI or fintech ecosystem and you:
🔹Want AI to sit 𝘪𝘯𝘴𝘪𝘥𝘦 your credit, risk, and collections workflows—not just in labs
🔹Are exploring agents but worried about RBI compliance, governance, and reliability
🔹Believe India’s stack (UPI, AA, OCEN, etc.) deserves a truly agentic AI layer on top
…I’d love to swap notes and share concrete architectures we’re seeing work on the ground.

Comment 𝐈𝐍𝐃𝐈𝐀 𝐅𝐈𝐍𝐓𝐄𝐂𝐇 𝐀𝐈 below or DM me, and I’ll send over patterns, reference designs, and examples tailored to Indian banking and fintech use cases.

#fintech #bfsi #aiinfinance #agenticworkflows #aiforindia

Crisil Integral IQ, an S&P Global company, has appointed Dipesh Doshi as Head of Global Capability Center (GCC), strengthening its leadership as it scales its global delivery and capability-building initiatives.

In this role, Mr. Doshi will lead the GCC business, driving delivery across risk, finance, credit, treasury, research, and operations functions. He will also oversee P&L, market development, and end-to-end onshore–offshore delivery, enabling scalable, cost-efficient outcomes for global financial institutions.

Mr. Doshi brings extensive experience in financial risk, regulatory compliance, and global advisory. Prior to joining CRISIL, he served as Partner – Financial Risk & Regulatory Compliance at PwC India, where he led large-scale regulatory and risk transformation programs for global financial institutions.

He has also held leadership roles at Protiviti as Managing Director – BFSI Risk & Compliance Consulting, and at Deloitte as Director – Financial Risk Management (FRM), building deep expertise across governance, risk, compliance, and GCC-led transformation models.

The team at CXO XPERTS wishes Mr. Dipesh Doshi continued success in his new role.

𝐑𝐁𝐈’𝐬 𝟏-𝐇𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐏𝐚𝐲𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐏𝐚𝐮𝐬𝐞 𝐂𝐨𝐮𝐥𝐝 𝐂𝐡𝐚𝐧𝐠𝐞 𝐃𝐢𝐠𝐢𝐭𝐚𝐥 𝐏𝐚𝐲𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐬 𝐅𝐨𝐫𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫.

The Reserve Bank of India has proposed a major shift:

A 𝐦𝐚𝐧𝐝𝐚𝐭𝐨𝐫𝐲 𝟏-𝐡𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐜𝐨𝐨𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐠-𝐨𝐟𝐟 𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐢𝐨𝐝 for digital transfers above ₹10,000 to a new beneficiary — giving banks and users time to detect and stop fraud before funds are withdrawn.

But that’s just one part of the proposal.
𝐀𝐥𝐬𝐨 𝐨𝐧 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐭𝐚𝐛𝐥𝐞:
• 𝐊𝐢𝐥𝐥 𝐒𝐰𝐢𝐭𝐜𝐡 — instant blocking of all digital transactions (UPI, net banking, cards)
• Extra authentication for transactions above ₹50,000 — especially to protect senior citizens and vulnerable users
• ₹𝟐𝟓 𝐥𝐚𝐤𝐡 𝐚𝐧𝐧𝐮𝐚𝐥 𝐜𝐫𝐞𝐝𝐢𝐭 𝐜𝐚𝐩 on accounts flagged as suspicious

At first glance, this looks like a consumer protection move.
But the operational impact runs much deeper.

𝐅𝐨𝐫 𝐛𝐚𝐧𝐤𝐬 & 𝐩𝐚𝐲𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐚𝐠𝐠𝐫𝐞𝐠𝐚𝐭𝐨𝐫𝐬:
Transaction staging, cancellation workflows, and real-time beneficiary risk scoring will become essential.

𝐅𝐨𝐫 𝐍𝐁𝐅𝐂𝐬 & 𝐟𝐢𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐜𝐡𝐬 (𝐢𝐧𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐧𝐭 𝐝𝐢𝐬𝐛𝐮𝐫𝐬𝐚𝐥𝐬):
This isn’t just compliance — it’s a product architecture challenge.

𝐅𝐨𝐫 𝐁𝐍𝐏𝐋 & 𝐝𝐢𝐠𝐢𝐭𝐚𝐥 𝐜𝐫𝐞𝐝𝐢𝐭 𝐩𝐥𝐚𝐭𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐦𝐬:
The ₹25 lakh cap could directly impact disbursals if onboarding risk profiling isn’t strong.

𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐚𝐥 𝐟𝐫𝐨𝐦 𝐑𝐁𝐈 𝐢𝐬 𝐜𝐥𝐞𝐚𝐫:
The industry is moving from educating users → to designing safer systems.

𝐀𝐫𝐞 𝐟𝐢𝐧𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐢𝐚𝐥 𝐢𝐧𝐬𝐭𝐢𝐭𝐮𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐬 𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐝𝐲 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐬 𝐬𝐡𝐢𝐟𝐭?

Follow KYCKART for insights on identity risk, onboarding gaps, and fraud prevention.

🚀 AI in Finance & Accounting: Transforming India’s Financial Ecosystem (2025–26)

Artificial Intelligence is no longer a future concept—it is actively reshaping how finance and accounting function across India. From banking to corporate finance, AI is driving a major shift from manual processes to intelligent, data-driven decision-making.

🔍 What’s changing?
• AI-led credit underwriting and risk assessment are redefining lending
• Automation in accounting is reducing errors and speeding up reporting
• Finance professionals are moving from bookkeeping to strategic roles
• AI-powered tools are enabling smarter personal finance decisions

📊 Impact on India’s ecosystem
• Significant productivity gains across BFSI and corporate sectors
• Rapid growth in AI-driven fintech and digital finance platforms
• Rising demand for skilled professionals in AI + finance
• Improved financial inclusion through smarter, faster services

⚠️ Challenges to address
• Data privacy and cybersecurity risks
• Regulatory and compliance complexities
• Need for large-scale upskilling and reskilling

💡 The bigger picture
AI is not replacing finance professionals—it is redefining their role. The future belongs to those who can combine financial expertise with technology and analytics.

India stands at a crucial point where AI can become a key driver of financial innovation, efficiency, and inclusive growth.

#AI #Finance #Accounting #FinTech #DigitalTransformation #India #FutureOfWork #Innovation

Explained: What the RBI's new reporting rule means for GIFT City funds. Dharmesh Trivedi of the AIF CFO Association, which represents 125 funds, explains in a conversation with Govindraj Ethiraj on The Core Report.

Here's what you need to know:

GIFT City just got its first foreign family office, three years after the framework was put in place. But a new RBI mandate is complicating things.

The Reserve Bank of India has mandated that alternative investment funds at the Gift IFSC must be treated as resident Indians when reporting foreign assets. This, Dharmesh Trivedi says, will lead to double reporting of the same money, leading to wrong numbers. Listen in!

⸻

🇮🇳 RBI’s NBFC Framework Reset: What’s Changing & Why It Matters

The Reserve Bank of India is recalibrating how NBFCs (Non-Banking Financial Companies) are classified and regulated — with a sharper focus on size, systemic risk, and function.

⸻

🔍 What’s Changing? (Core Shift)

* Move to a scale-based regulatory framework (SBR)
* Classification anchored primarily on asset size
* Functional oversight retained (customer interface, public funds usage, etc.)
* Aim: Simplify + tighten supervision where risk is higher

⸻

🧱 New 4-Tier Structure

1. Base Layer (BL) – Smaller NBFCs
2. Middle Layer (ML) – Moderate size & activity
3. Upper Layer (UL) – Systemically important NBFCs
4. Top Layer (TL) – Potential high-risk entities (added dynamically)

⸻

🔢 Vital Numbers

* ₹1 Lakh Crore → Threshold for Upper Layer NBFCs
* Top 10 NBFCs → Automatically fall into UL
* 70% Weight → Quantitative scoring (size, leverage)
* 30% Weight → Qualitative factors (complexity, interconnectedness)
* Review Cycle → Every 5 years (even if size changes)

⸻

⚙️ Why This Matters

* NBFCs don’t take savings deposits like banks
* Increasing role in:
    * Retail credit
    * Microfinance
    * Infrastructure financing
* Growth of fintech + shadow banking risks
* Lessons from 2008 global crisis → need tighter oversight

⸻

🔄 Functional Regulation Focus

* Customer interface
* Public funds exposure
* Market linkages
* Group structures & leverage

👉 RBI ensures risk-based supervision, not just size-based

⸻

📊 Evolving NBFC Landscape

* Rise of:
    * Digital lenders
    * Account aggregators
    * Specialized financing firms
* Increasing interconnectedness with banks
* Government-backed infra financing pushing sectoral depth

⸻

⚖️ Proposed vs Current Approach

* Earlier: Heavy reliance on functional classification
* Now: Hybrid model
    * Size-driven layering
    * Functional risk overlays
* Possible de-registration of small ‘family office’-type NBFCs

⸻

🗓️ Timeline Snapshot

* Post-2008 → Stronger financial sector regulations globally
* 2021–2023 → RBI introduces SBR framework
* 2024–2026 → Refinement of UL/TL classification & thresholds
* Next Phase → Continuous monitoring + dynamic reclassification

⸻

🎯 RBI’s End Goal

* Strengthen financial stability
* Improve risk detection early
* Ensure proportionate regulation
* Avoid systemic contagion

⸻

💡 Bottom Line

India’s NBFC sector is no longer peripheral — it’s core to credit delivery.
RBI’s framework ensures growth without fragility.

⸻

#RBI #NBFC #FinancialRegulation #IndiaEconomy #Banking #Fintech #RiskManagement #CorporateGovernance #CreditMarkets

posts.content:
RBI Fixes a Problem It Created

Over the past month, the RBI tightened forex rules to control speculation in the rupee.
But in doing that, it accidentally made normal banking operations harder.

Now, it is partially rolling that back.

* Net Open Position (NOP) limit unchanged at $100 million per bank
* Back-to-back hedging now allowed, even with related parties
* Existing positions can be held till maturity, no forced unwinding
* Applies across domestic and overseas branches
* Comes after rupee fell ~11% in FY26

> Control stays, but flexibility returns

What Was the Real Issue

Banks don’t just trade currencies to speculate. Most of the time, they hedge risk.

For example, a bank may take a position in India and offset it through its overseas branch. This is called a back-to-back hedge. It reduces risk, not increases it.

But earlier rules started treating even these as speculative if they involved related entities.

> That created a mismatch.

Banks were being penalized for reducing risk.

So RBI has now clarified the intent:
Hedging = allowed
Speculation = restricted

This distinction is critical in forex markets.

Why This Matters More Than It Looks

Forex markets run on liquidity and risk transfer.

If banks cannot hedge freely:

Risk stays on their books
Costs increase
Market liquidity reduces

By allowing genuine hedging again, RBI is ensuring the system keeps functioning smoothly without opening the door to speculation.

At the same time, keeping the $100 million cap ensures that overall risk-taking stays controlled.

The Bigger Signal

This is a classic regulatory balance.

First tighten to stop misuse.
Then fine-tune to avoid damaging real activity.

> It also shows that policy is now more responsive to market feedback.

Bottom Line

RBI is not loosening control, it is refining it, allowing banks to manage risk efficiently while still keeping speculation in check, which is essential for a stable and functional currency market.

Phoenix Legal’s Financial Regulatory newsletter reports the Reserve Bank of India’s recent issuance of the Master Direction – Reserve Bank of India (Unique Identifiers in Financial Markets) Directions, 2026, which consolidates prior circulars and establishes a unified framework for the implementation of Legal Entity Identifiers (“LEI”) and Unique Transaction Identifiers (“UTI”) across financial markets.

The Master Direction introduces a phased implementation timeline, with LEI requirements effective from March 27, 2026, and UTI obligations commencing from January 1, 2027. It mandates the use of LEIs for a wide range of OTC transactions and high-value foreign exchange dealings, reinforcing transparency and data standardisation across market participants. In parallel, the UTI framework establishes a standardised mechanism for identifying and tracking OTC derivative transactions throughout their lifecycle, supported by a structured “waterfall mechanism” to determine the UTI-generating entity.

Given RBI’s emphasis on standardisation, traceability, and regulatory reporting, market participants may wish to reassess their internal systems, documentation practices, and transaction workflows to ensure timely compliance with the evolving identifier regime in India’s financial markets.

Authored by Radhika Raman(Partner) and Sudhanshu Singh(Associate).

#Phoenixlegal #RBI

Why does this “silent scam” need to be exposed?

It needs to be exposed because its true scale is hidden in plain sight and changing ethical and value based practices in #Corporates fast and the culprits are Reserve Bank of India (RBI) Indian Ministry of Finance Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India #Banks #NBFC #TRAI 

Individually, these charges may appear insignificant—₹300 here or ₹700 there.

 A penalty for not maintaining minimum balance, an auto-debit bounce fee, a partially settled mediclaim presented as a full settlement, or persistent prepaid recharge reminders just days before expiry.

 Each instance feels minor, almost ignorable. But that is precisely what makes it dangerous.

When such practices operate at a national scale, impacting tens of crores of Indians, the cumulative effect becomes enormous. 

If even 50 crore people are compelled to pay ₹300–₹700 in various charges and penalties, the total extraction runs into tens of thousands of crores. 

What seems trivial to an individual becomes massive, systemic gain for institutions.

And this is not just about money.


It raises a deeper concern,  the normalization of unethical practices under the cover of regulation. 

When institutions that are expected to protect consumers—regulators, financial bodies, and oversight authorities—either permit, overlook, or indirectly enable such practices, it creates an ecosystem where #accountability weakens and trust erodes.

The real question, therefore, is not about the amount being charged.

It is about the principle involved.

Should corporations and financial institutions be allowed to build profits on practices that are technically permissible but ethically questionable?

Should systems meant to safeguard citizens instead become silent participants in their financial erosion?

If these patterns go unchallenged, they gradually redefine what is acceptable. 

Over time, exploitation becomes policy, and inconvenience becomes revenue.
Exposing this “silent scam” is not about outrage—it is about restoring balance.

It is about ensuring that transparency, fairness, and integrity are not optional values, but foundational ones.

Because when small injustices are multiplied at scale, they stop being small.

🏦 RBI Partially Eases Rupee Derivative Trade Curbs | Forex Market Update

📌 What Happened:

The Reserve Bank of India has partially rolled back restrictions on rupee derivative trades — curbs that were introduced earlier this month to prevent the rupee from hitting successive record lows.
🔍 Key Developments:

✅ What's Now Allowed

Authorised dealers (banks & financial institutions) can once again offer Non-Deliverable Forwards (NDFs) to both Indian residents and overseas users
Ban on rebooking cancelled foreign exchange derivative contracts has been lifted

❌ What Still Remains Restricted

Authorised dealers cannot enter into rupee-related forex derivative contracts with their related parties
Exceptions limited to: cancellation/rollover of existing contracts & back-to-back transactions with unrelated non-residents

📖 Background:

On April 1, RBI had directed authorised dealer banks to discontinue offering non-deliverable derivative contracts involving the Indian rupee — aimed at curbing speculative activity and enhancing transparency as the rupee came under pressure from West Asia conflict tensions.

📊 Market Impact:

The April 1 curbs had briefly boosted the rupee from below ₹95/$ to ₹93.10 — a sharp rally of ₹1.73
On Monday, rupee settled at ₹93.12/$, paring early gains by 21 paise amid continued dollar demand driven by West Asia tensions and firm crude prices

💡 The Bigger Picture:

RBI's calibrated partial rollback signals a regulatory recalibration — tightening oversight without completely restricting legitimate hedging activity. The message is clear: speculation will be curbed, but genuine forex risk management must continue.

Money Changing in India Is Under Much Deeper Audit Scrutiny Than Most Realize 💱

India’s forex ecosystem—spanning AD1 banks, AD2 entities, and FFMCs—handles $700B+ in annual flows (trade + remittances). But what’s often underestimated is the intensity of audit and compliance layers behind every transaction.

📊 Here’s what the audit landscape really looks like:

🔍 1. Concurrent Audit Is No Longer Optional

100% mandatory for FFMCs and AD2s Transaction-level coverage in many cases For AD1 banks, embedded within real-time risk monitoring systems 

👉 Result: Most forex transactions are effectively pre-audited, not post-audited

📉 2. Cash Risk Is Tightly Controlled

₹50,000 cap per transaction (cash) PAN mandatory beyond thresholds Structuring detection is a key audit trigger 

👉 In practice, >90% of flagged audit observations in money changing relate to cash handling & KYC gaps

📑 3. Reporting Discipline Is Ruthless

Monthly RBI reporting (T+10 timeline) for FFMCs Large transaction monitoring (USD 10,000+) Suspicious Transaction Reports (STR) escalation via FIU 

👉 Even minor delays can trigger regulatory escalation or penalties

🏢 4. Franchisee Networks = Hidden Risk Layer

Mandatory half-yearly audits + surprise inspections Currency surrender timelines (typically ≤7 days) Strict prohibition on unauthorized selling 

👉 Over 30–40% of compliance gaps are typically observed at franchisee level, not head office

⚖️ 5. Multi-Layer Audit Stack

Concurrent audit (real-time) Internal audit (process validation) Statutory audit (annual certification) RBI inspection (risk-based supervision) 

👉 This creates a 4-layer defense system for forex compliance

🚨 What Auditors Actually Flag Most:

Incomplete KYC / travel documentation Transaction structuring below thresholds Cash vs system mismatch Delayed or inaccurate RBI reporting 

Bottom line:
Money changing in India is no longer a simple buy/sell business—it’s a highly audited, data-tracked financial activity where compliance capability is as critical as distribution.

The firms that scale sustainably will be those that treat audit readiness as a core operating capability—not a back-office function.

posts.author_name: Delhi HC slams indiscriminate freezing of bank accounts; Calls for policy reform in cyber-crime investigations | Ravindra Krishnappa
posts.content:
Banks putting legitimate accounts on HOLD is getting to be an epidemic. 

Inspite of multiple recommendations and rulings by the Honourable high courts of Delhi, Kerala etc, banks continue to put a "blanket" hold on company current accounts for either frivilous or seemingly small value credits from dubious sources. 
So, for the sake of a small unsubstantiated claim, EVERYONE ie. employeees, the vendors, government agencies like PT, PF, GST, TDS etc. - everyone is blocked. Basically, business grinds to halt and legitimate transactions are being held to ransom.
This needs URGENT action !
Ministry Of Home Affairs (mha), GOI, Ministry Of Corporate Affairs, Reserve Bank of India (RBI), HDFC Bank

The Court particularly criticized the blanket freezing of accounts based on minuscule transactions linked to cyber-crimes. It pointed out that instead of freezing entire accounts, law enforcement should explore alternative measures such as marking a lien on the specific disputed amount. Relying on Pawan Kumar Rai v. Union of India, 2024 SCC OnLine Del 8936, where it had previously ruled that freezing an entire account without any indication that the account holder was complicit in cyber-crime violated fundamental rights, including the right to livelihood under Article 21 of the Constitution.

The Court remarked that “the possibility of marking a lien on disputed amount, whenever it is identifiable, should be explored as a more appropriate interim measure. Ideally, it should be the first and foremost option. This would, naturally, mitigate the undue hardship being caused on account of blanket freezing of account and would also ensure that the alleged cheated money remains secured and intact.”

Additionally, the Court referred to similar observations made by the Kerala High Court in Dr. Sajir v. Reserve Bank of India, 2023 SCC OnLine Ker 9087 and Abdul Basith v. Cyber, Economic & Narcotic Crime, 2025 SCC OnLine Ker 83, wherein it was held that unless an account holder was proven to be complicit in a crime, their entire bank account should not be frozen merely because a suspicious transaction was traced to it.

One complaint. Just one. That is all it takes.

56% of MFDs in our study cannot produce complete risk profiling records within 30 minutes if SEBI asks.

This is not a future risk. This is a present reality.

Under SEBI's March 2026 Master Circular, every MFD must maintain timestamped, documented risk profiling records for every client — available for inspection at any time.

The myth: "SEBI will go after the big fish. I am safe."
The reality: SEBI/AMFI compliance checks are triggered by a SINGLE investor complaint. Your AUM size does not protect you. Your geography does not protect you. Your years of experience do not protect you.

What protects you is documentation.

Our study across 101 MFDs in 64 cities found:
→ 42% have ZERO formal documentation of risk profiling
→ 52% conduct risk profiling verbally — no scoring, no record
→ 84% do not provide written rationale for recommendations routinely

Do you know where YOUR practice stands?

Take the 5-minute survey. Get your personal compliance readiness score instantly.

India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (DPDP) Act is reshaping how data fiduciaries, processors, and the government handle our personal information.
From consent and erasure to breach notifications and penalties — the law empowers individuals (Data Principals) while holding organizations accountable.

Key pillars of the DPDP framework:
✅ Individuals – Consent, access, correction, deletion
✅ Data Fiduciaries – Health providers, e-commerce, telecom, startups, regulators
✅ Data Processors – IT vendors, AI/analytics firms, outsourcing companies
✅ Government – Complaints & grievances
✅ Data Protection Board of India – Investigation, penalties, breach handling, guidance

Rights & responsibilities go hand in hand.
- Compliance + data security = trust.
- A big step toward a privacy-aware digital India.

posts.content:
DPDP Act Decoded #36: Governance and Reporting Expectations — When You Are a Significant Data Fiduciary

When your organisation is designated as a Significant Data Fiduciary, privacy stops being only a legal or compliance function.

Under the DPDP framework, it acquires a clear governance dimension.

The clearest signal is in how the Act designs the Data Protection Officer role.

For a Significant Data Fiduciary, the DPO must be an individual based in India, represent the organisation under the Act, be responsible to the Board of Directors or a similar governing body, and act as the point of contact for grievance redressal.

This is not just an operational appointment.
It is a statutory governance linkage.

A Significant Data Fiduciary must also appoint an independent data auditor, and undertake periodic Data Protection Impact Assessments and periodic audits.

The Rules make this more concrete.
These must be undertaken once in every period of twelve months from the date on which the entity is notified as a Significant Data Fiduciary, and the person carrying out the DPIA and audit must furnish to the Board a report containing significant observations.

That means privacy oversight is not confined to internal compliance teams.
The framework expressly creates reporting into the Board.

The Rules also require the Significant Data Fiduciary to observe due diligence to verify that technical measures, including algorithmic software adopted for hosting, display, uploading, modification, publishing, transmission, storage, updating or sharing of personal data processed by it, are not likely to pose a risk to the rights of Data Principals.

That is a statutory verification obligation.
In practice, it also makes technical and product risk a governance subject.

For founders, the Act does not create a separate statutory category of founder obligations.

But where founders sit on, control, or function through the similar governing body, these obligations become relevant in practice.

A prudent governance model would therefore ensure that the governing body receives clear reporting on DPIA findings, audit observations, technical-risk verification, and any transfer restrictions that may be notified for specified personal data and related traffic data.

Rule 13(4) is not a general localisation mandate.
But where it is triggered, it can create direct architectural and cross-border data flow consequences.

The practical question is not whether controls exist on paper.

It is whether the Significant Data Fiduciary can demonstrate that privacy oversight reaches the top of the organisation through the governance mechanisms the Act and Rules require.

Relevant Provisions

DPDP Act, 2023
 • Section 2(z)
 • Section 10(1)
 • Section 10(2)(a)
 • Section 10(2)(b)
 • Section 10(2)(c)(i)
 • Section 10(2)(c)(ii)

DPDP Rules, 2025
 • Rule 13(1)
 • Rule 13(2)
 • Rule 13(3)
 • Rule 13(4)

#DPDPAct #DataProtection #BoardGovernance #AIGovernance #PrivacyByDesign #DPDPA #DPDP

The "ghosts" in your corporate machine might be your biggest legal liability. 👻 As Indian organizations race to comply with the 𝑫𝒊𝒈𝒊𝒕𝒂𝒍 𝑷𝒆𝒓𝒔𝒐𝒏𝒂𝒍 𝑫𝒂𝒕𝒂 𝑷𝒓𝒐𝒕𝒆𝒄𝒕𝒊𝒐𝒏 (𝑫𝑷𝑫𝑷) 𝑨𝒄𝒕, one silent threat stands out: 𝑺𝒉𝒂𝒅𝒐𝒘 𝑰𝑻.

𝐖𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐢𝐬 𝐒𝐡𝐚𝐝𝐨𝐰 𝐈𝐓? 🕵️‍♂️
Shadow IT refers to any software, cloud service, or hardware used by employees for work without explicit approval from the IT or Security department. Think of that "quick" file conversion site, an unauthorized project management tool, or even an AI chatbot used to process sensitive client data.

𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐏𝐞𝐫𝐢𝐥𝐬 𝐮𝐧𝐝𝐞𝐫 𝐃𝐏𝐃𝐏 ⚖️
The DPDP Act places the burden of safety squarely on the Data Fiduciary (the organization). Here’s why Shadow IT is a ticking time bomb:

𝑪𝒐𝒏𝒔𝒆𝒏𝒕 & 𝑵𝒐𝒕𝒊𝒄𝒆 𝑩𝒓𝒆𝒂𝒄𝒉𝒆𝒔: Under the Act, personal data must be processed only for a "lawful purpose" with clear consent. If an employee puts data into an unvetted app, that data is being processed without a valid notice or consent framework. 🚫📜

𝑻𝒉𝒆 "𝑹𝒊𝒈𝒉𝒕 𝒕𝒐 𝑬𝒓𝒂𝒔𝒖𝒓𝒆" 𝑵𝒊𝒈𝒉𝒕𝒎𝒂𝒓𝒆: Data Principals (users) have the right to ask for their data to be deleted. If your IT team doesn't even know the data exists on a random SaaS platform, how can you fulfill this legal request? 📑❌

𝑫𝒂𝒕𝒂 𝑩𝒓𝒆𝒂𝒄𝒉 𝑽𝒖𝒍𝒏𝒆𝒓𝒂𝒃𝒊𝒍𝒊𝒕𝒚: Shadow IT tools often lack enterprise-grade encryption. Under DPDP, failing to take "reasonable security safeguards" to prevent a breach can lead to penalties of up to ₹250 Crore. 💸😱

𝒁𝒆𝒓𝒐 𝑨𝒖𝒅𝒊𝒕 𝑻𝒓𝒂𝒊𝒍𝒔: The Act requires accountability. If a data leak occurs through an unmonitored channel, your organization won't have the logs or trails needed to report the incident accurately to the Data Protection Board (DPB). 🔍🚫

𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐁𝐨𝐭𝐭𝐨𝐦 𝐋𝐢𝐧𝐞 🛡️
In the era of DPDP, ignorance is no longer a defense. Organizations must move from "detecting" Shadow IT to "governing" it.

𝑨𝒖𝒅𝒊𝒕 𝒚𝒐𝒖𝒓 𝒔𝒕𝒂𝒄𝒌: Identify every tool currently in use. 📊

𝑬𝒅𝒖𝒄𝒂𝒕𝒆 𝒚𝒐𝒖𝒓 𝒕𝒆𝒂𝒎: Help them understand that a "handy" app could lead to a massive compliance failure. 🎓

𝑷𝒓𝒐𝒗𝒊𝒅𝒆 𝒔𝒂𝒇𝒆 𝒂𝒍𝒕𝒆𝒓𝒏𝒂𝒕𝒊𝒗𝒆𝒔: If employees are straying, it’s usually because the official tools are too slow or clunky. ⚡

Stay compliant, stay secure! 🔒✨

#DPDPAct #DataPrivacy #ShadowIT #CyberSecurity #IndiaTech #DataProtection #Compliance #InfoSec #PrivacyFirst #CorporateGovernance

Leadership Update | Redacto

Redacto has announced the appointment of M Jaya krishna as its Chief Business Officer (CBO), marking a significant step in strengthening its leadership team as demand for privacy infrastructure continues to grow in India...

In this role, Jaya will lead Redacto’s business development and digital marketing functions end-to-end, with a focus on driving enterprise adoption at a time when organisations are preparing for the requirements of the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act...

Commenting on the appointment, Amit K., Co-Founder and CEO, Redacto, said, “ I am excited to have Jaya join our leadership team as we shape the next chapter of Redacto’s growth and impact.”

Jaya Krishna added, “Indian enterprises are facing a real compliance gap, and Redacto has built a platform designed from the ground up to address it. Privacy today is a foundational challenge, and the opportunity to build a category-defining solution in this space is both exciting and critical.”

Wishing him all the best in his new role.

Read more here: https://lnkd.in/gMFKJ3Md

𝗘𝗺𝗽𝗼𝘄𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗖𝗜𝗦𝗢 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗺𝘂𝗻𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗜𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗮’𝘀 𝗗𝗣𝗗𝗣 𝗘𝗿𝗮 🇮🇳

I was honored to deliver the keynote address at the CISO India Connect 2026 in Bangalore, organized by EXE Globe. My session focused on a critical shift: 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗡𝗲𝘄 𝗘𝗿𝗮 𝗼𝗳 𝗖𝘆𝗯𝗲𝗿 𝗔𝗰𝗰𝗼𝘂𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗗𝗣𝗗𝗣 𝗔𝗰t.

As we move from theoretical compliance to practical enforcement, I delved into:
🔹 The salient features and stringent penalty framework of the Act.
🔹 The "Accountability Matrix" – clearly defining the roles of the Board of Directors, DPO, CISO, and Senior Management.
🔹 Why "Reasonable Security Safeguards" are now a legal mandate, not just a technical goal.

A highlight of the event was hearing from the Chief Guest, Dr. MA Saleem, IPS, DG & IGP of Karnataka. His insights into the Government’s initiatives and the pivotal role of the Centre for Cybercrime Investigation Training & Research (CCITR) were incredibly valuable for all security leaders present.

It was also a privilege to present the Cyber Security Excellence Awards to a group of truly deserving CISOs. Your dedication to securing the digital frontier is inspiring! 🏆

Great to catch up with Lalit Gupta "The Cyber Doctor"—your session was as insightful as always.

Thank you, Madhumita Ramadugula, for the invitation and the opportunity to contribute to this august gathering.

#DPDPAct #CyberSecurity #CISOIndia #DataPrivacy #CyberAccountability #Leadership #learning #thankful | 13 comments on LinkedIn

𝐈 𝐫𝐞𝐯𝐢𝐞𝐰𝐞𝐝 𝐚 𝐒𝐞𝐫𝐢𝐞𝐬 𝐁 𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐫𝐭𝐮𝐩'𝐬 𝐜𝐨𝐝𝐞𝐛𝐚𝐬𝐞 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐃𝐏𝐃𝐏 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐥𝐢𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐞


Neither the legal team had any idea nor the engineering team.
What I was going to find. So, here is the report.

𝐅𝐢𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐠 1: 𝐒𝐢𝐧𝐠𝐥𝐞 𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐮𝐬𝐞𝐫 𝐈𝐃 
1️⃣What the code said: One ID. Linked to every feature, every integration, every log file. 
2️⃣What DPDP says: Comprehensive personal profile. Every use of that profile requires a separate legal basis.
3️⃣Legal basis documented: None. 
4️⃣Status: Non-compliant.

𝐅𝐢𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐠 2: 𝐋𝐨𝐠 𝐫𝐞𝐭𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐬𝐞𝐭 𝐭𝐨 𝐢𝐧𝐝𝐞𝐟𝐢𝐧𝐢𝐭𝐞 
1️⃣What the code said: Logs never auto-purge. Engineering team deletes manually when storage gets tight. 
2️⃣What DPDP says: Retention beyond the stated purpose violates storage limitation. 
3️⃣What CERT-In says: You must retain for 180 days minimum. 
4️⃣Status: Non-compliant in two directions simultaneously.

𝐅𝐢𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐠 3: 𝐅𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐒𝐃𝐊𝐬 𝐭𝐫𝐚𝐧𝐬𝐦𝐢𝐭𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐨𝐧𝐚𝐥 𝐝𝐚𝐭𝐚 
1️⃣What the code said: Mixpanel, Firebase, Crashlytics, Segment. Standard stack. 
2️⃣What DPDP says: Each one is a Data Processor and requires a written Data Processing Agreement. 
3️⃣DPAs in place: Zero. 
4️⃣Status: Four uncontracted processors.

𝐅𝐢𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐠 4: 𝐔𝐬𝐞𝐫_𝐢𝐝 𝐨𝐧 𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐲 𝐚𝐧𝐚𝐥𝐲𝐭𝐢𝐜𝐬 𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐧𝐭 
1️⃣What the code said: user_id attached to every event for attribution accuracy. 
2️⃣What DPDP says: The analytics vendor is now processing personal data on your behalf. 
3️⃣DPIA conducted: No. 
4️⃣Status: Uncontracted Data Processor. Unassessed risk.

𝐅𝐢𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐠 5: 𝐃𝐞𝐥𝐞𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐯𝐢𝐚 𝐬𝐨𝐟𝐭 𝐟𝐥𝐚𝐠 𝐨𝐧𝐥𝐲 
1️⃣What the code said: is_deleted = true. Simple. Reversible. 
2️⃣What DPDP says: Data present in production database, three backup environments, data warehouse, analytics pipeline, and six third-party integrations. 
3️⃣Status: Erasure right cannot be fulfilled as the law requires.
___________________________________________
👉 The legal team asked one question when they saw the report: "Who approved all of this?"

👉 The engineering team gave the only honest answer available: "Nobody asked us not to."

🙌 That answer is the entire problem.
And it is sitting in your codebase right now.

🤙 Discuss it with me and i am happy to guide. Book FREE 1:1 (Check comments)

#DPDP #PrivacyByDesign #Engineering #DataPrivacy #CyberLaw #CTO #Compliance #WCSF

𝗠𝘆𝘁𝗵𝗼𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗕𝗮𝗻𝗸𝗲𝗿: 𝗔 𝗣𝗿𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗲𝗿'𝘀 𝗩𝗶𝗲𝘄 𝗼𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗜 𝗧𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗿𝗶𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗜𝗠𝗙

𝗗𝗮𝘆 𝟯 𝗼𝗳 𝟳 | 𝗣𝗮𝗽𝗲𝗿 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗿𝗼𝗹𝘀 𝘃𝘀. 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗿𝗼𝗹𝘀. 𝗪𝗵𝗶𝗰𝗵 𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘀 𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝗵𝗼𝗹𝗱?

Every bank has a control framework that looks impressive on paper.

Policies, procedures, matrices, RACI charts, attestations, sign-offs. Thousands of documented controls, mapped to regulations, mapped to risks, mapped to owners. Audit-ready. Regulator-ready. Board-ready.

Then something like Mythos shows up, and you quietly ask yourself a different question.

𝙒𝙝𝙞𝙘𝙝 𝙤𝙛 𝙩𝙝𝙚𝙨𝙚 𝙘𝙤𝙣𝙩𝙧𝙤𝙡𝙨 𝙖𝙘𝙩𝙪𝙖𝙡𝙡𝙮 𝙬𝙤𝙧𝙠?

I've sat through enough control self-assessments to know the answer is uncomfortable. Many controls exist because they were required once, documented once, and never re-examined. They sit in the framework because removing them is harder than keeping them. The control owner signs off each quarter because signing off is what the process asks for.

This is the difference between a paper control and a real control.

𝘼 𝙥𝙖𝙥𝙚𝙧 𝙘𝙤𝙣𝙩𝙧𝙤𝙡 𝙞𝙨 𝙤𝙣𝙚 𝙩𝙝𝙖𝙩 𝙬𝙤𝙪𝙡𝙙 𝙛𝙖𝙞𝙡 𝙪𝙣𝙙𝙚𝙧 𝙜𝙚𝙣𝙪𝙞𝙣𝙚 𝙥𝙧𝙚𝙨𝙨𝙪𝙧𝙚, 𝙗𝙪𝙩 𝙣𝙤𝙗𝙤𝙙𝙮 𝙝𝙖𝙨 𝙩𝙚𝙨𝙩𝙚𝙙 𝙞𝙩 𝙧𝙚𝙘𝙚𝙣𝙩𝙡𝙮 𝙚𝙣𝙤𝙪𝙜𝙝 𝙩𝙤 𝙠𝙣𝙤𝙬.

𝘼 𝙧𝙚𝙖𝙡 𝙘𝙤𝙣𝙩𝙧𝙤𝙡 𝙞𝙨 𝙤𝙣𝙚 𝙩𝙝𝙖𝙩 𝙬𝙤𝙪𝙡𝙙 𝙝𝙤𝙡𝙙, 𝙗𝙚𝙘𝙖𝙪𝙨𝙚 𝙨𝙤𝙢𝙚𝙤𝙣𝙚 𝙝𝙖𝙨 𝙖𝙘𝙩𝙪𝙖𝙡𝙡𝙮 𝙩𝙧𝙞𝙚𝙙 𝙩𝙤 𝙗𝙧𝙚𝙖𝙠 𝙞𝙩.

Mythos, or any AI model of its class, is a test of that distinction. It doesn't care what your policy document says. It doesn't care that the control is signed off. 𝙄𝙩 𝙥𝙧𝙤𝙗𝙚𝙨 𝙗𝙚𝙝𝙖𝙫𝙞𝙤𝙪𝙧, 𝙣𝙤𝙩 𝙙𝙤𝙘𝙪𝙢𝙚𝙣𝙩𝙖𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣.

And here is where practitioners in risk and control functions have to be honest with themselves. A lot of what we call assurance is documentation hygiene. Checking that the evidence exists. Checking that the sign-off happened. Checking that the review was performed. These are necessary. They are not sufficient.

Real assurance asks a harder question. If an adversary tried this specific control today, would it hold?

For many controls, we genuinely do not know. And the honest reason we don't know is that the testing cadence was set when adversaries were slower, rarer, and less capable.

Three questions worth taking into your next risk committee.

Which of our key controls have been genuinely stress-tested in the last twelve months, not just reviewed?

Which controls exist primarily because the framework requires them, rather than because they demonstrably reduce risk?

If we removed the bottom twenty percent of our controls by actual effectiveness, would the risk profile change at all?

The answers are often uncomfortable. The conversation is worth having anyway.

Tomorrow. The three lines of defence at AI speed.

#SaurabhSays #Mythos #RiskAndControl #Assurance #InternalAudit

🌟 𝐌𝐞𝐦𝐛𝐞𝐫 𝐢𝐧 𝐅𝐨𝐜𝐮𝐬: Luthra & Luthra LLP, Chartered Accountants

At the SME Chamber of India, we spotlight organizations that combine deep expertise with proven institutional credibility.

We are pleased to feature Luthra & Luthra LLP Chartered Accountants, a firm known for delivering end-to-end financial, taxation, and advisory solutions backed by decades of experience and a strong professional team.

With a team of 150+ professionals including CA, CS, ICWA, and legal experts, the firm offers a one-stop solution across assurance, taxation, corporate law, ESG, and insolvency services helping businesses navigate complex regulatory and financial landscapes.

Leading the firm’s advisory strength is Akhilesh Gupta, Senior Partner, who brings over 35 years of rich experience in management consulting, taxation, and restructuring.

His expertise spans:
 • Business Restructuring & Turnaround Strategies
 • IFRS / Ind AS Implementation
 • Domestic & International Taxation
 • Indirect Taxation & GST Advisory
 • Corporate Law, RBI & FEMA Matters

He has represented clients before key regulatory and judicial bodies including CBDT, ITAT, DRP, NCLT, NCLAT and other authorities ensuring strong compliance and strategic positioning.

As a registered Insolvency Professional under the Insolvency & Bankruptcy Code, 2016, he plays a vital role in advising distressed businesses and structuring revival and resolution frameworks.

What further strengthens Luthra & Luthra LLP’s credibility is its empanelment with premier institutions such as:
 • Comptroller & Auditor General of India
 • Reserve Bank of India
 • National Highways Authority of India
 • Income Tax and GST Departments (Special Audits)
 • National Investigation Agency
 • All Leading Indian Banks
 • Leading international embassies and institutions

The firm’s approach is rooted in creating long-term value through strategic advisory, deep domain knowledge, and client-centric engagement, enabling businesses to scale with confidence.

We are proud to have Luthra & Luthra LLP as part of our esteemed network.

A new chapter begins🎉 🥳 

Excited to share that I have started my CA Articleship at Walker Chandiok & Co LLP in the Statutory Audit domain.✨

Officially entered the world of audits, balance sheets, and infinite Excel sheets! 

From studying concepts in books to now applying them in real-world scenarios, this transition feels both exciting and challenging. I’m looking forward to learning from practical exposure, developing a problem-solving mindset, and growing every day in this journey.✨

Looking forward to working on diverse assignments, enhancing my analytical skills, and building a strong professional foundation.

A heartfelt thanks to everyone who supported and guided me in finding and securing my articleship—your encouragement truly means a lot.

posts.content:
We are proud to recognize Karthik Jagannathan, a dynamic and results-driven Internal Audit Leader with extensive experience in audit, finance, risk management, and internal controls across diverse industries.

Currently, Karthik Jagannathan serves as Manager at Protiviti Middle East Member Firm in Bahrain, where he focuses on internal audit, risk assessment, and strengthening governance frameworks. In this role, he supports organizations in enhancing internal controls, ensuring compliance with IFRS standards, and driving effective risk management practices.

Previously, Karthik Jagannathan held senior leadership roles including Financial Controller at Dadabhai Construction and Senior Finance Manager at Havelock AHI W.L.L., where he managed financial operations, reporting, and strategic planning. He also served as Partner at B B S K and Associates and held key audit leadership roles at BDO Bahrain as Senior Audit Manager and Audit Manager, delivering high-quality audit and assurance services.

Earlier in his career, Karthik Jagannathan built a strong foundation through roles such as Tax Manager at EY, Manager – Finance & Administration at Crane Bank Limited, and Audit Assistant at Deloitte Haskins & Sells, gaining deep expertise in taxation, financial reporting, and audit practices.

Karthik Jagannathan is a Chartered Accountant from The Institute of Chartered Accountants of India and holds globally recognized certifications including Certified Information Systems Auditor (ISACA) and Certified Internal Auditor (IIA). He also earned a Bachelor of Commerce (BCom) from Annamalai University, further strengthening his foundation in finance and accounting.

👏 We celebrate Karthik Jagannathan achievements, leadership, and his impactful contributions to audit excellence and financial governance.
